# Intermediate Microeconomics Teaching Prep Timeline {-}
**Course Start:** August 24, 2026  
**Today:** March 10, 2026  
**Preparation Period:** ~24 weeks

## Guiding Principles
- **Efficiency First:** Leverage publisher slides + colleague materials extensively
- **Research Priority:** Front-load administrative tasks, minimize summer disruption
- **Build Gradually:** Spread preparation to avoid cramming
- **Interactive Teaching:** Work through examples, give students practice time (not lecture-only)
- **Use Bookdown as Knowledge Base:** Build course website gradually as you prep

---

## PHASE 1: IMMEDIATE TASKS (March 9-16, 2026)
**Focus:** Handle urgent administrative items + gather all materials

### Week of March 9-16
- [x] **DONE: Textbook order submitted to OU bookstore!** ✅
  - Textbook: Microeconomics (4th ed.) by Goolsbee, Levitt, and Syverson
  - Inclusive Access option selected ✅
  - Achieve online access included ✅
  
- [ ] **Request materials from colleague** ✅ (Already received!)
  - Review all attachments they sent
  - Organize files into folders by chapter
  - Note: They modify publisher's instructor slides (you'll do the same!)
  
- [ ] **Request publisher instructor resources from Macmillan**
  - Contact your Macmillan Learning rep or go to their instructor portal
  - Request: Instructor slides for all chapters (2-9)
  - Request: Test bank, solution manuals, additional resources
  - These are FREE for verified instructors
  
- [ ] **Access Glen Weyl materials**
  - Download/organize his lecture slides
  - Bookmark YouTube playlist: https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PLDcm2lSo_eYw_QD9mdW1HWD5pesgEzvHl&si=VmQNdC5ytFvDGx2b
  - Review to see chapter alignment with Goolsbee text

- [ ] **Obtain Hal Varian textbook** (optional reference)
  - Get copy of "Intermediate Microeconomics: A Modern Approach"
  - You used this as a student - familiar resource!
  - Useful for:
    - Comparing pedagogical approaches
    - More mathematical treatment of topics
    - Alternative explanations for difficult concepts
    - Practice problems with different style
  - Can recommend to students as supplementary resource

- [ ] **Learn Canvas basics as instructor** (2-3 hours)
  - Log into your Canvas instructor account
  - Take OU's Canvas instructor training (if available)
  - Or watch Canvas instructor tutorials on YouTube:
    - "Canvas for Teachers: Getting Started"
    - "How to Set Up Your Canvas Course"
  - Familiarize yourself with:
    - Creating modules
    - Uploading files
    - Creating assignments
    - Setting up gradebook
    - Making announcements
    - Using Canvas calendar
  
- [ ] **Learn about Achieve-Canvas LTI integration** (1-2 hours)
  - Read Macmillan's guide: "Integrating Achieve with Canvas"
  - Watch Achieve integration tutorial videos
  - Key concepts to understand:
    - LTI (Learning Tools Interoperability) - how external tools connect to Canvas
    - Single Sign-On (SSO) - students access Achieve through Canvas automatically
    - Grade passback - Achieve sends grades directly to Canvas gradebook
    - Roster sync - Canvas automatically shares student roster with Achieve
  - **Why this matters:** Students will access ALL homework through Canvas, never going to Achieve directly

- [ ] **Bookdown Website Setup** (2-3 hours)
  - Review your existing site: https://vmooers.github.io/intermediatemicro/
  - Plan bookdown structure for chapters (these will appear in ToC):
    ```
    index.Rmd (homepage/syllabus)
    01-introduction.Rmd
    02-supply-and-demand.Rmd
    03-using-supply-demand.Rmd
    04-consumer-behavior.Rmd
    05-individual-market-demand.Rmd
    06-producer-behavior.Rmd
    07-costs.Rmd
    08-perfect-competition.Rmd
    09-market-power-monopoly.Rmd
    10-resources.Rmd (links to external resources)
    ```
  
  - Create separate folder for materials NOT in ToC:
    ```
    /static/
      /slides/ (lecture slides by chapter)
      /exams/ (practice exams, study guides)
      /homework/ (bonus problems, practice sets)
      /data/ (datasets for examples)
      /images/ (figures, graphs)
    ```
  
  - **Strategy:**
    - Write chapter content in bookdown (your notes, key concepts, examples)
    - Link to slides/materials in `/static/` from within chapters
    - Can keep items unlinked until ready to release
    - Example: In `02-supply-and-demand.Rmd`, add: 
      ```markdown
      ## Lecture Materials
      - [Lecture Slides (PDF)](/static/slides/ch02-slides.pdf)
      - [Practice Problems](/static/homework/ch02-practice.pdf)
      ```

---

## PHASE 2: SPRING SEMESTER PREP (March 17 - May 15, 2026)
**Focus:** Light preparation while prioritizing research

### Late March (March 17-31)
- [ ] **Read the entire textbook** (Chapters 1-9 of Goolsbee)
  - Budget: 2 chapters per week = ~4-5 hours/week
  - Take notes on key concepts, difficult sections
  - Flag areas where you'll need extra prep
  - **Compare with Varian** where helpful:
    - Varian is more mathematical/abstract
    - Goolsbee is more applied/empirical
    - Use Varian for rigorous proofs students might ask about
    - Use Goolsbee for intuition and real-world examples
  
  - **Bookdown task:** Start drafting chapter notes in your .Rmd files
    - Write key concepts in your own words
    - Add examples that resonate with you
    - Note difficult sections that need extra attention
    - This becomes your personal study guide!
  
- [ ] **Review colleague's syllabus thoroughly**
  - Decide what policies to keep vs. modify
  - Consider: grading weights, attendance policy, office hours
  - Draft list of changes you want to make
  - Note their teaching format: "work through lots of examples in class and give students time to work on their own"

### April (Weeks of April 1-30)
- [ ] **Continue textbook reading** (complete by end of April)
  
- [ ] **Review materials week by week** - ONE CHAPTER PER WEEK
  - Week 1: Chapter 2 (Supply & Demand)
    - Review colleague's modified slides
    - Review publisher's original slides
    - Watch Weyl lecture if available
    - Check Varian's treatment (Chapter 1 in Varian)
    - Note which examples work well
    - ~3-4 hours
  
  - Week 2: Chapter 3 (Using Supply & Demand)
  - Week 3: Chapter 4 (Consumer Behavior) - key chapter!
    - Varian Chapters 2-6 cover utility, preferences, choice
    - Compare mathematical approaches
  - Week 4: Chapter 5 (Individual & Market Demand)
  
  **For each chapter:**
  - Understand the flow and key concepts
  - Identify which examples to keep/modify/add
  - Note difficult concepts that need extra time
  - Think about in-class practice problems
  - Compare Goolsbee vs. Varian approaches
  
- [ ] **Bookdown Website Development** (30-45 min/week)
  - Add to your chapter .Rmd files as you review materials
  - Include:
    - Key concepts and definitions
    - Your favorite examples
    - Common student confusions you anticipate
    - Links to helpful resources (Weyl videos, etc.)
  - Don't worry about polish - this is your personal knowledge base
  - Can render locally to see how it looks

### Early May (May 1-15)
- [ ] **Complete lecture material review** (Chapters 6-9)
  - Chapter 6: Producer Behavior (Varian Ch 18-19: Technology, Profit Maximization)
  - Chapter 7: Costs (Varian Ch 20: Cost Minimization)
  - Chapter 8: Perfect Competition (Varian Ch 22: Industry Supply)
  - Chapter 9: Market Power & Monopoly (Varian Ch 24: Monopoly)
  - Same review process as April chapters
  
- [ ] **Review all 4 exams from colleague**
  - Understand difficulty level, question types
  - Check solutions for accuracy
  - Note which problems you find challenging
  - Consider using publisher test bank for additional questions
  - Compare problem styles: Goolsbee vs. Varian problems

- [ ] **Canvas/Achieve integration deep dive** (3-4 hours)
  - **Critical to understand before summer!**
  - Log into Achieve as instructor
  - Review pre-built assignments for each chapter
  - Practice adding custom questions
  - Practice deleting questions you don't like
  - Understand grading/feedback features
  - **Test the Canvas integration:**
    - Request a Canvas sandbox course from OU IT (if available)
    - Or use your actual Canvas course in setup mode
    - Install Achieve LTI app in Canvas
    - Link a test assignment
    - Complete it as a student (test student view)
    - Verify grade passes back to Canvas gradebook
  - **Document the process for yourself:**
    - Screenshot each step
    - Note any quirks or issues
    - Create troubleshooting guide
  - Watch Macmillan's webinars on Canvas integration if available

- **End of Phase 2 Checkpoint:**
  - ✅ Textbook fully read (Goolsbee + referenced Varian)
  - ✅ All existing materials reviewed  
  - ✅ Publisher slides obtained
  - ✅ Clear sense of what needs modification
  - ✅ Bookdown structure in place with draft content
  - ✅ Canvas/Achieve integration understood and tested

---

## PHASE 3: SUMMER BREAK - RESEARCH FOCUS (May 16 - July 31, 2026)
**Focus:** Minimal teaching prep, maximize research productivity

### May 16 - June 30 (6 weeks)
**RESEARCH INTENSIVE PERIOD - Minimal teaching work**

- [ ] **One small task per week** (1-2 hours max):
  - Week 1 (May 16-22): Draft your own syllabus
    - Use colleague's syllabus as template
    - Incorporate teaching format: examples + student practice time
    - Decide on office hours schedule
    - Add Weyl videos as optional supplementary resource
    - Consider adding Varian as optional textbook for math-oriented students
  
  - Week 2 (May 23-29): Finalize syllabus decisions
    - Grading breakdown (homework via Achieve)
    - Attendance policy  
    - Late work policy
    - Canvas/Achieve access instructions for students
    - Add note: "All homework accessed through Canvas"
  
  - Week 3 (May 30-June 5): Plan first day of class
    - Icebreaker activity?
    - Syllabus review approach
    - Canvas/Achieve walkthrough for students (important!)
    - First substantive topic to cover
  
  - Week 4 (June 6-12): Canvas/Achieve preliminary setup
    - Set up basic Canvas course structure
    - Install Achieve LTI integration
    - Set deadlines for first 2-3 assignments
    - Write Canvas announcement with Achieve access instructions
    - Test again to ensure it works
  
  - Week 5 (June 13-19): Bookdown website light work
    - Review and edit existing chapter drafts
    - Add any new insights from your review process
    - Don't worry about completeness - iterative process!
  
  - Week 6 (June 20-26): **FULL RESEARCH WEEK** - no teaching prep!

### July 1-31 (4 weeks)
**BEGIN MORE INTENSIVE PREP**

- [ ] **Week 1 (July 1-7): Syllabus & Course Structure**
  - Finalize syllabus (3-4 hours)
  - Create detailed class schedule/calendar for Fall 2026
  - Plan pacing: how many class periods per chapter?
  - Draft first day welcome email
  - **Create Canvas/Achieve student guide** (1-2 hours):
    - Step-by-step instructions with screenshots
    - "How to Access Homework Through Canvas"
    - "What to Do If You Can't Access Achieve"
    - Common troubleshooting issues
    - Who to contact for tech support
  - **Bookdown:** Add finalized syllabus to index.Rmd

- [ ] **Week 2 (July 8-14): Chapters 2-3 Slides**
  - Start with publisher's instructor slides as base
  - Modify with your own examples (current events, OU-specific)
  - Add in-class practice problems (with time for students to work)
  - Remove content that's too advanced or doesn't fit your time
  - Reference Varian for students who want deeper mathematical treatment
  - Practice delivering 2-3 lectures out loud
  - **Teaching format reminder:** Plan where to pause for student practice
  - Time estimate: 6-8 hours
  - **Bookdown:** 
    - Upload final slides to `/static/slides/`
    - Link from chapter pages
    - Add practice problems to `/static/homework/`

- [ ] **Week 3 (July 15-21): Chapters 4-5 Slides**
  - Same process as Chapters 2-3
  - Consumer theory - crucial material, needs extra examples
  - Identify concepts that often confuse students
  - Create multiple practice problems for in-class work
  - Consider Varian's more mathematical approach for advanced students
  - Time estimate: 6-8 hours
  - **Bookdown:** Upload materials and update links

- [ ] **Week 4 (July 22-31): Chapters 6-7 Slides**  
  - Producer theory - mirror your consumer theory approach
  - Parallel structure helps students see connections
  - Time estimate: 6-8 hours
  - **Bookdown:** Upload materials and update links

---

## PHASE 4: FINAL PREP (August 1-23, 2026)
**Focus:** Complete preparation, system setup, mental readiness

### Week 1 (August 1-7): Final Chapters + Canvas Complete Setup

- [ ] **Customize Chapters 8-9 slides** - 6-8 hours
  - Perfect competition and monopoly
  - Strong examples make these chapters memorable
  - **Bookdown:** Upload materials

- [ ] **Canvas course complete setup** - 6-8 hours
  - **Critical week for Canvas/Achieve integration!**
  
  **Course Structure:**
  - Upload finalized syllabus and schedule
  - Create modules (one per chapter):
    ```
    Module 1: Introduction & Syllabus
    Module 2: Supply & Demand (Ch 2)
    Module 3: Using Supply & Demand (Ch 3)
    Module 4: Consumer Behavior (Ch 4)
    ... etc.
    ```
  
  **Achieve Integration:**
  - Install Achieve LTI app in Canvas (if not done in May)
  - For each chapter module:
    - Add Achieve homework assignment as "External Tool"
    - Set due date (typically Sunday after chapter completion)
    - Set point value
    - Enable grade passback
  - **Test every single assignment link** as student!
  - Verify all grades flow correctly to gradebook
  
  **Gradebook Setup:**
  - Create assignment groups:
    - Homework (20%) - from Achieve
    - Exam 1 (20%)
    - Exam 2 (20%)
    - Exam 3 (20%)
    - Exam 4 (20%)
  - Configure weighted grading
  - Add your attendance adjustment mechanism
  - Test calculations with dummy grades
  
  **Student Resources:**
  - Create "Course Resources" page:
    - Link to Achieve access instructions
    - Link to Weyl YouTube playlist (optional supplement)
    - Note about Varian textbook (optional, more mathematical)
    - Link to your bookdown site (if sharing)
    - Office hours and contact info
  
  **Communication Setup:**
  - Create announcement templates:
    - Weekly reminders about homework deadlines
    - Exam reminders
    - Office hours reminders
  - Set up Canvas calendar with all deadlines
  - Schedule first week announcements
  
  **First Week Materials:**
  - Post syllabus
  - Post Chapter 2 materials
  - Post first homework assignment (with clear instructions)
  - Post Canvas/Achieve access guide
  - Create welcome announcement with video intro (optional)

- [ ] **Test Canvas course thoroughly** (1-2 hours)
  - Use Student View feature in Canvas
  - Click every link
  - Try to access every Achieve assignment
  - Follow your own instructions
  - Ask a colleague or friend to test if possible
  - Fix any broken links or confusing navigation

### Week 2 (August 8-14): Exam Preparation

- [ ] **Review and customize all 4 exams** - 6-8 hours
  - Start with colleague's exams as template
  - Supplement with publisher test bank questions
  - Consider adding Varian-style problems for mathematical rigor
  - Ensure they match your lecture emphasis
  - Update any dated examples
  - Create answer keys
  - **Create Canvas quiz shells** for each exam:
    - Even though in-person, Canvas helps with recording grades
    - Can post solutions after exam through Canvas
  
- [ ] **Finalize Exam 1 (Chapters 2-3)** - 2-3 hours
  - This is your most immediate need
  - Create both exam and solutions document
  - Consider creating one alternative version
  - Upload to Canvas (but keep hidden until exam day)
  
- [ ] **Prepare bonus problems** for Chapters 2-3 - 2 hours
  - Review colleague's bonus problems
  - Modify or create your own
  - Prepare solutions
  - Decide: distribute paper or post to Canvas?
  - **Bookdown:** Can post these in `/static/homework/` with links

### Week 3 (August 15-23): Final Touches & Practice

- [ ] **August 17-18: Faculty orientation** (attend all sessions)
  - Great opportunity to meet other new faculty
  - Learn about OU systems and resources
  - Ask about Canvas support resources
  - Ask about classroom technology
  
- [ ] **Practice teaching** - 6-8 hours total
  - Do a full "dress rehearsal" of first 3-4 lectures
  - Practice the interactive format:
    - Work through example
    - Give students 5-10 min to try similar problem
    - Discuss solutions
  - Time yourself (aim for ~45 min content, leaving 5 min for Q&A)
  - Record yourself if possible to check pacing
  - Practice board work and transitions
  - Practice using any classroom technology (projector, document camera, etc.)
  - **Practice explaining Canvas/Achieve access** - students WILL be confused!
  
- [ ] **Achieve final checks** - 1-2 hours
  - Review first 3 homework assignments one more time
  - Ensure questions align with what you'll cover
  - Delete any problematic questions
  - Verify due dates make sense
  - Create answer guide for yourself (so you can help students)
  
- [ ] **Canvas final checks** - 1-2 hours
  - Review entire course from student perspective
  - Is navigation intuitive?
  - Are instructions clear?
  - Are all deadlines visible on calendar?
  - Is everything that should be published, published?
  - Is everything that should be hidden, hidden?
  - Test all Achieve assignment links one more time
  
- [ ] **Administrative final prep** - 3-4 hours
  - Review roster, start learning student names
  - Prepare first day materials:
    - Printed syllabus (some students prefer paper)
    - Name tents or icebreaker activity
    - Canvas/Achieve access handout (paper backup!)
    - First homework assignment details
    - **Print backup slides** (in case technology fails)
  - Create "Week 1 checklist" for yourself
  - Set up email folders/labels for student communication:
    ```
    ECON 3113
      ├── Homework Questions
      ├── Grade Questions  
      ├── Absence Notifications
      └── Tech Issues (Canvas/Achieve)
    ```
  - Plan office hours (add to calendar, reserve room if needed)
  - Prepare for common Canvas/Achieve questions:
    - "I can't access Achieve"
    - "My homework isn't showing up"
    - "My grade isn't showing in Canvas"
    - Have OU IT help desk number ready: (405) 325-HELP
  
- [ ] **Bookdown final touches** - 1-2 hours
  - If sharing with students: 
    - Final review of all public content
    - Remove any TODO notes or incomplete sections
    - Render and test all links
    - Add resources page with Weyl videos, Varian reference, etc.
  - If keeping private: 
    - Ensure all your notes are organized and accessible
    - Render locally so you can reference during teaching
  - Either way: This is ongoing - you'll keep updating as you teach!

- [ ] **Self-care & contingency prep** - 2 hours
  - Review first-time teacher advice
  - Prepare "emergency backup plans":
    - Extra practice problems if class ends early
    - Backup topics if you run out of material
    - Plan B if Canvas goes down (rare but possible)
    - Plan B if Achieve goes down (have offline homework ready?)
  - Organize your teaching materials:
    - Binder with all syllabi, handouts
    - Digital files organized and backed up
    - USB drive with all slides (backup)
  - Prepare your office space for student visitors
  - **Get good sleep August 22-23!**
  - **Have coffee/tea ready for August 24 morning** ☕

### August 24: First Day of Class! 🎉

- **First Day Checklist:**
  - [ ] Arrive 20 minutes early
  - [ ] Test projector and computer
  - [ ] Write your name and email on board
  - [ ] Write office hours on board
  - [ ] Have printed syllabus ready
  - [ ] Have Canvas/Achieve handout ready
  - [ ] Take attendance (start learning names!)
  - [ ] Introduce yourself (mention this is your first time teaching this course - builds rapport!)
  - [ ] Cover syllabus (don't rush - students need to understand policies)
  - [ ] **Demo Canvas/Achieve access** (walk through on projector)
  - [ ] Mention optional resources (Weyl videos, Varian, bookdown site if public)
  - [ ] If time: start Chapter 2 content
  - [ ] Stay after class for questions
  - [ ] Send welcome email with Canvas/Achieve instructions (again!)

---

## TOTAL TIME ESTIMATES BY PHASE

| Phase | Time Period | Est. Hours/Week | Total Hours |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Phase 1 | March 9-16 | 8-10 hours | 8-10 hours |
| Phase 2 | March 17-May 15 (9 weeks) | 5-6 hours | 45-55 hours |
| Phase 3 | May 16-June 30 (6 weeks) | 1-2 hours | 6-12 hours |
| Phase 3 | July 1-31 (4 weeks) | 6-8 hours | 24-32 hours |
| Phase 4 | August 1-23 (3 weeks) | 10-12 hours | 30-36 hours |
| **TOTAL** | | | **~115-145 hours** |

**Average: 5-6 hours per week over 24 weeks**

---

## DETAILED CANVAS/ACHIEVE INTEGRATION GUIDE

### Understanding the Integration

**What is LTI?**

- Learning Tools Interoperability - standard protocol for external tools to integrate with Canvas
- Achieve uses LTI to connect with Canvas
- Once set up, students never need to log into Achieve separately

**How it Works:**

1. OU bookstore sets up student Achieve access (through Inclusive Access)
2. You install Achieve LTI app in your Canvas course
3. You link Achieve assignments to Canvas modules
4. Students click assignment in Canvas → automatically logged into Achieve
5. Students complete homework in Achieve
6. Grades automatically sync to Canvas gradebook
7. You can view student progress in both Canvas and Achieve instructor dashboard

**Benefits:**

- ✅ Students access everything through one place (Canvas)
- ✅ Automatic grading - saves you hours per week
- ✅ Grades automatically in Canvas gradebook
- ✅ Students can't claim they "couldn't find" homework
- ✅ Built-in due date reminders through Canvas

### Common Issues & Solutions

**Issue:** Students can't access Achieve

- **Check:** Did they opt out of Inclusive Access? (Need to purchase separately)
- **Check:** Did they click through Canvas, not go to Achieve directly?
- **Solution:** Have them contact OU bookstore to reinstate Inclusive Access

**Issue:** Grades not showing in Canvas

- **Check:** Did you enable grade passback when creating assignment?
- **Check:** Is Achieve assignment worth points in Canvas?
- **Solution:** Edit assignment settings, ensure "Sync Grades" is enabled

**Issue:** Student can't see assignment

- **Check:** Is module published?
- **Check:** Is assignment published?
- **Check:** Are there date restrictions on the assignment?

**Issue:** Assignment link doesn't work

- **Check:** Did Achieve app lose connection? May need to reinstall LTI
- **Solution:** Go to Settings → Apps → Find Achieve → Reinstall

### Resources for Canvas/Achieve
- Macmillan support: https://macmillan.force.com/macmillanlearning/s/
- Canvas guides: https://community.canvaslms.com/
- OU Canvas help: https://canvas.ou.edu/
- OU IT Help Desk: (405) 325-HELP (4357)

---

## BOOKDOWN STRUCTURE RECOMMENDATIONS

### What Goes in Bookdown Chapters (public/table of contents):

**index.Rmd** - Homepage

- Course title and description
- Your info and contact
- Office hours
- Link to full syllabus (PDF)
- Getting started guide

**01-introduction.Rmd**

- What is microeconomics?
- Course overview
- How to succeed in this course
- Resources available

**02-supply-and-demand.Rmd** through **09-market-power-monopoly.Rmd**

For each chapter:

- Key concepts and definitions
- Important graphs/figures
- Worked examples (your favorites)
- Common mistakes to avoid
- Links to lecture slides: `[Week 3 Slides](/static/slides/ch02-week3.pdf)`
- Links to practice problems: `[Practice Set 2](/static/homework/ch02-practice.pdf)`
- Links to Weyl videos: `[Watch Prof. Weyl's lecture](...)`

**10-resources.Rmd**

- Optional textbooks (Varian)
- YouTube playlists (Weyl)
- Practice problem sources
- Study tips
- OU tutoring resources

### What Goes in /static/ (not in table of contents):

```
/static/
  /slides/
    ch02-week1.pdf
    ch02-week2.pdf
    ch03-week3.pdf
    ...
  /homework/
    ch02-practice.pdf
    ch02-practice-solutions.pdf
    ch02-bonus.pdf
    ...
  /exams/
    exam1-practice.pdf
    exam1-practice-solutions.pdf
    study-guide-exam1.pdf
    ...
  /data/
    supply-demand-example.csv
    monopoly-example.xlsx
  /images/
    graphs, figures, etc.
```

### Bookdown Tips

**Keeping Things Private:**

- Can have draft chapters (don't list in `_bookdown.yml`)
- Can link to materials in `/static/` only when ready
- Example: Have exam solutions but don't link until after exam
- Can have private branch for your notes, public branch for student site

**Iterative Development:**

- Don't need to finish everything before launching
- Can add chapters as semester progresses
- Can update content weekly
- Students appreciate seeing it grow with the course

**Best Practice:**

- Write in your own voice - this is your interpretation of the material
- Include examples that resonate with you
- Note where Goolsbee vs. Varian differs
- Add current events/applications
- Keep it concise - students won't read walls of text

---

## KEY RECOMMENDATIONS

### On Canvas/Achieve Integration

**MOST IMPORTANT:** 

- Test the integration thoroughly in May
- Test again in August before students arrive
- Have clear troubleshooting guide ready
- First day of class: walk through access on projector
- Send Canvas/Achieve instructions via email on Day 1
- Be patient - there will be tech issues week 1

**Time savers:**

- Achieve does automatic grading
- Grades sync to Canvas automatically
- No need to manually enter homework grades!
- Can see student progress without asking

### On Using Publisher Materials

✅ **DO:**

- Start with publisher's instructor slides for every chapter
- Modify them freely - add your examples, remove fluff
- Use publisher test bank for exam questions
- Leverage their pre-built Achieve homework assignments
- Customize freely - this is expected and encouraged!

❌ **DON'T:**

- Feel guilty about using these materials (you paid for them!)
- Try to create slides from scratch
- Use their materials without any modification
- Ignore their instructor resources

### On Interactive Teaching Format
Your colleague's advice: **"Students responded best to a course format where I work through lots of examples in class and give students time to work on their own rather than a lecture-only format."**

**Implementation tips:**

- Plan 2-3 worked examples per class period
- After each example, give a similar problem for students to try (5-10 min)
- Walk around, answer questions while they work
- Discuss solutions together
- This makes 50-minute classes fly by!
- **Time management:** Don't over-prepare content - interactive time fills quickly

**Typical class structure:**

1. Brief concept review (10 min)
2. Work through Example 1 (10 min)
3. Students practice similar problem (7 min)
4. Discuss solution (5 min)
5. Work through Example 2 (10 min)
6. Students practice (7 min)
7. Wrap-up/questions (3 min)

### On Hal Varian Textbook

**How to use it:**

- **For you:** Deeper mathematical understanding, alternative explanations
- **For students:** Recommend as optional supplement for math-oriented students
- **In class:** "If you want to see a more mathematical treatment, check Varian Chapter X"
- **On exams:** Can include more mathematical problems (but also more applied ones)

**Key differences:**

- Varian: More abstract, proof-based, mathematical rigor
- Goolsbee: More applied, empirical examples, real-world focus
- **Your approach:** Use Goolsbee as main text, reference Varian for deeper theory

### On Glen Weyl Materials

**You do NOT need permission to:**

- Use his public YouTube videos as supplementary resources
- Recommend them to students as optional viewing
- Watch them yourself for pedagogical ideas
- Use pedagogical approaches you observe

**Best approach:** 

- List Weyl videos as "optional supplementary lectures" on syllabus/bookdown
- Link to them in relevant chapters
- Students appreciate having alternative explanations
- Send courtesy email thanking him (optional but nice)

### On Bookdown Development

**Keep it simple your first time:**

- Think of it as your personal course notebook
- Write chapter notes as you prep
- Add materials gradually
- Link to resources as you find them
- Don't stress about making it perfect

**Decide: Public or Private?**

- **Public:** More pressure to polish, but students appreciate organized resources
- **Private:** Can be messy, work-in-progress, just for you
- **Recommendation:** Start private, can make public later after first semester

### Research Protection Strategy

🔬 **June is your RESEARCH MONTH** - protect it fiercely

- Most intensive teaching prep: April-May (reading/reviewing) and July-August (customizing)
- June 20-26: Complete break from teaching prep
- Total prep time is ~115-145 hours over 24 weeks
- That's only 5-6 hours/week average - very manageable!

### First-Timer Tips
1. **Week 1 will feel chaotic** - totally normal
2. **Canvas/Achieve will confuse students** - plan for this
3. **Arrive 15 minutes early** every class (first 2 weeks)
4. **Prepare 55-60 min of material** for 50-min classes initially
5. **Have backup activities** ready (extra practice problems)
6. **Record yourself** teaching (with permission) in Week 1-2
7. **Be transparent:** "This is my first time teaching this course"
8. **Interactive format is FORGIVING** - can adjust on the fly

### What You DON'T Need to Do
- Memorize everything (you can use notes!)
- Make Canvas look fancy (functional > pretty)
- Respond to emails instantly (48 hours is fine)
- Know every answer immediately
- Make slides from scratch (use publisher slides!)
- Create all new exam questions (use test bank)
- Finish bookdown before semester starts (iterative!)

---

## EMERGENCY SHORTCUTS (if timeline gets compressed)

If something happens and you fall behind, here's priority order:

**CRITICAL (Must have):**

1. Syllabus completed ✅ 
2. Canvas course set up with Achieve integrated
3. First 2 weeks of lectures ready (Chapters 2-3)
4. First 3 Achieve homework assignments checked and linked
5. Exam 1 prepared
6. Canvas/Achieve student guide created

**HIGH PRIORITY:**

7. All lecture slides reviewed (even if not fully customized)
8. First 4 weeks of material ready
9. Understand Achieve system thoroughly
10. Practice teaching interactive format

**NICE TO HAVE:**

11. All lectures fully customized
12. All exams prepared in advance
13. Bonus problems created
14. Bookdown site polished
15. Varian references integrated

**SKIP IF NECESSARY:**

16. Bookdown public site (keep private for yourself)
17. Recording yourself practicing
18. Creating alternative exam versions
19. Fancy Canvas design

---

## MONTHLY CHECKLIST SUMMARY

### March ✅
- ✅ Submit textbook order (DONE!)
- Collect all materials (colleague + publisher)
- Learn Canvas basics
- Learn Canvas/Achieve integration
- Set up bookdown structure
- Start reading textbook

### April 📚
- Finish textbook reading (Goolsbee, reference Varian)
- Review Chapters 2-5 materials
- Watch some Weyl lectures for ideas
- Add notes to bookdown as you go
- Compare different textbook approaches

### May 🔍
- Review Chapters 6-9 materials
- Review all 4 exams
- Test Canvas/Achieve integration thoroughly
- Complete material review

### June 🔬
- Light admin tasks (1-2 hrs/week max)
- **PRIMARY FOCUS: RESEARCH**
- One week completely off teaching prep

### July 💼
- Finalize syllabus and schedule
- Customize lecture slides (using publisher as base)
- Plan interactive elements
- Upload materials to bookdown
- Create Canvas/Achieve student guide

### August 🎯
- Canvas complete setup
- Achieve integration finalized and tested
- All assignments linked and tested
- Exam preparation
- Practice teaching (especially interactive format)
- Faculty orientation
- Final prep and self-care

---

## CONFIDENCE BUILDERS

Remember:

- ✅ You have a PhD in Economics - you deeply understand this material
- ✅ 40 students is very manageable
- ✅ You have excellent materials: colleague's + publisher's + Weyl's + Varian
- ✅ Canvas/Achieve automates grading (huge time saver!)
- ✅ Intermediate Micro is well-structured and standardized
- ✅ Students are forgiving of first-time teachers who are prepared
- ✅ You have 5+ months - plenty of time!
- ✅ Interactive format is easier than pure lecture
- ✅ Bookdown gives you flexible personal knowledge base
- ✅ Textbook order is already done! ✅

### Reality Check on Time Investment
- **Total prep time:** ~115-145 hours over 24 weeks
- **Average per week:** 5-6 hours
- **Compare to:** Many new instructors spend 200+ hours prepping from scratch
- **Your advantage:** Great materials + systematic timeline + Canvas/Achieve automation

### What Success Looks Like (First Time)
- Students learn the material ✅
- Canvas/Achieve integration works smoothly ✅
- You don't panic before each class ✅
- Exams are fair and aligned with lectures ✅
- You survive and want to teach again ✅

You don't need:

- ❌ Perfect lectures
- ❌ Zero Canvas/Achieve issues (there will be some)
- ❌ To be the best teacher students ever had
- ❌ To revolutionize pedagogy
- ❌ To compete with experienced faculty

### First Semester Goals (Realistic)
1. **Cover all required material** ✓
2. **Students pass at reasonable rates** ✓
3. **Canvas/Achieve works for homework** ✓
4. **Interactive teaching format engages students** ✓
5. **Get through without major disasters** ✓
6. **Learn what works and what doesn't** ✓
7. **Improve for next time** ✓

**You've absolutely got this!** 🎓📊✨

---

## APPENDIX: QUICK REFERENCE

### Important Contacts
- OU Bookstore: soonerbooks@textbookbrokers.com
- OU IT Help Desk: (405) 325-HELP (4357)
- Macmillan Support: https://macmillan.force.com/macmillanlearning/s/
- Canvas Help: https://canvas.ou.edu/

### Key Resources
- Textbook: Goolsbee, Levitt, Syverson, 4th ed.
- Optional: Varian, "Intermediate Microeconomics"
- Weyl Videos: https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PLDcm2lSo_eYw_QD9mdW1HWD5pesgEzvHl
- Your Bookdown: https://vmooers.github.io/intermediatemicro/

### Week 1 Must-Haves (Day 1 Checklist)
- [ ] Printed syllabus
- [ ] Canvas/Achieve handout
- [ ] Test projector/tech
- [ ] Student roster
- [ ] First lecture slides
- [ ] Backup plan if tech fails
- [ ] Office hours schedule posted
- [ ] Canvas fully tested
- [ ] Good night's sleep!
- [ ] Coffee ☕

### Emergency "I'm Behind" Priority List
1. Canvas/Achieve working
2. Syllabus done
3. First 2 weeks lectures ready
4. Exam 1 ready
5. Student access guide ready
6. Everything else can wait

**Good luck - you're going to do great!** 🎉
